key_insight: SCION is an agentic "operating system" for scientific discovery that coordinates tasks, tools, memory, and verification so research can be executed more like a structured workflow than a collection of isolated AI tools.
clinical_relevance: For drug discovery, this could help researchers more efficiently generate, screen, and refine therapeutic hypotheses, but it is a research-enabling platform rather than a clinical decision tool.
method: Turns high-level scientific intent into a Research Execution Plan with staged goals, dependencies, checkpoints, and fallback paths.
Uses hierarchical multi-agent execution with specialized profiles, selective context building, governed delegation, and layered memory.
Frames discovery as target-conditioned inverse search, including batch active search when the target is hidden and experiments are limited.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports evaluations across materials analysis, molecule design, protein/antibody screening, and scientific reading/idea/molecule generation, with SCION outperforming baseline autonomous research agents on decomposition, verification, refinement, and memory reuse.
watch_item: Performance gains depend on robust task planning, tool integration, and memory governance, so real-world reliability and reproducibility may vary outside the reported benchmarks.
paper_title: Rethinking Scientific Discovery in an Agentic Era
